topic: Theme 3: Immigration and the Multicultural French Society
subject: french

items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).

items.q: How do you say 'I play the piano' vs 'I play football'?
items.correct: Je joue DU piano (instrument) vs je joue AU football (sport).
items.misconception: Same preposition for both - 'je joue piano', 'je joue football'.
items.random_wrong: Je joue le piano, je joue le football.
items.why: Students drop or confuse prepositions. The rule: jouer DE + instrument (de, du, de la); jouer À + sport/game (à, au, aux). Easy way to lose marks in speaking exams about hobbies.

items.q: Which French verb form correctly says 'I would have helped' (j'... aidé)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: aurais. “aurai” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which conjunction always triggers the subjunctive?
items.why: Correct answer: bien que. “parce que” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many subjunctive 'triggers' do you typically learn at A-Level (il faut que, je veux que, bien que, pour que, sans que, à condition que)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 triggers. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
